summary: DHH vents about struggling with a hopelessly broken requirement specification for a major client. The spec is inconsistent, varying wildly between extreme detail on trivial matters and vague one-liners on important features. He argues the only sensible option is to start over, but management refuses because too much time has already been invested. He's frustrated by his junior position, which leaves him powerless to influence the decision.
key_points: Badly written requirement specs mix wildly varying levels of detail, making them incoherent
Sunk cost fallacy drives organizations to patch broken documents rather than start fresh
Junior developers can clearly see systemic problems but lack the organizational power to fix them
Prioritizing a client signature over specification quality leads to meaningless busywork
money_quotes: Everything molded together in a steaming pot of incomprehensibility.
It's beyond salvation, but the powers that be are not at all interested in reaching that conclusion.
Too much time has been spend already, we need the signature, so work have degenerated into patching and padding insignificant details.
Starting over (the only sensible option at this point) is out of the question.
At the bottom of the hierarchy nobody can hear you scream.
key_insight: Organizations often cling to broken specifications due to sunk cost fallacy, choosing to patch the unsalvageable rather than start over, while those closest to the problem lack the authority to force the right decision.
